source.title: Enzyme engineering: A synthetic biology approach for more effective library generation and automated high-throughput screening.

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to develop a versatile method for generating and screening smart enzyme libraries to engineer Candida antarctica lipase A (Cal-A) for altered substrate selectivity, specifically discrimination between short-chain and long-chain fatty acid triglycerides. Traditional methods suffer from high cost, experimental difficulty, time-consuming steps, and lack of flexibility in recombining mutations. The Golden Gate strategy using type IIS restriction enzymes is proposed to enable seamless, unidirectional assembly of independently mutated gene fragments, combined with an automated whole-cell screening assay.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: The Golden Gate strategy uses type IIS restriction enzymes for seamless recombination of DNA fragments. Hot-spots for mutations were identified in three regions of Cal-A. Gene segments were mutated using targeted or random methods, then reassembled to create diverse libraries. An automated screening method was developed to visually discriminate hydrolysis of short-chain vs long-chain fatty acid substrates by whole cells.
process_signals: Hot-spots individuated in three distinct regions of Cal-A
Three gene segments subjected to most appropriate mutagenesis method
Straightforward reassembly allowed combining products of different mutagenesis methods in a single round
Screening aided by development of a general, automated method for visual discrimination
methods: Golden Gate assembly with type IIS restriction enzymes
Targeted and random mutagenesis of gene segments

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Effective mutagenesis requires small, targeted, high-quality 'smart' libraries due to screening constraints. Simultaneous mutations can have non-additive effects. Existing methods have disadvantages: high cost, experimental demand, time, and inflexibility in recombining mutations. Golden Gate assembly is proposed to overcome these by allowing independent mutagenesis of gene segments and seamless recombination. Cal-A is chosen as a model enzyme due to its unique properties and industrial potential for discriminating short-chain vs long-chain fatty acids. The substrate binding mode is undefined, so a smart library targeting the putative binding area is needed.
process_signals: Need for creative solutions to explore combinatorial complexity
Existing methods suffer from disadvantages: high cost, experimentally demanding, time-consuming, lack flexibility
Golden Gate strategy inspired by synthetic biology applications
Cal-A chosen for its unique properties and industrial relevance
Hypothesis: targeting tunnel region and key residues may modulate substrate specificity
methods: Smart library design combining targeted and random mutagenesis
failures_or_limits: Existing methods: high cost, experimental demand, time, inflexibility
Substrate binding mode of Cal-A remains undefined

section_gists.heading: Library design
section_gists.gist: Smart library design based on structural analysis: tunnel region (residues 211-350) targeted for random mutagenesis; residues Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431 selected for NDT saturation mutagenesis. The gene was divided into three parts for independent mutagenesis and reassembled using Golden Gate with BsaI and SapI. This allows combining random and focused mutagenesis, exploring synergistic effects. Ten libraries were generated with various combinations of mutated and native parts. The method enables easy recombination and flexibility.
process_signals: Tunnel region hypothesized to bind substrate, targeted for random mutagenesis
Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431 selected based on proximity to catalytic triad and structural analysis
NDT degeneracy chosen for good chemical diversity with modest library size
Gene divided into three parts for independent mutagenesis
Golden Gate assembly with BsaI and SapI for seamless recombination
Ten libraries generated with different combinations
methods: Golden Gate assembly with BsaI and SapI
failures_or_limits: Care needed to avoid unwanted BsaI/SapI sites in sequence

section_gists.heading: Screening
section_gists.gist: An automated screening assay was developed using a liquid-handler robot to inoculate saturated cultures onto agar plates with emulsified triglycerides (tributyrin for C4, olive oil/rhodamine for C18). Active clones produce halos (clear or fluorescent). The method is qualitative but robust and reproducible. Screening of library 10 revealed variants with altered selectivity: some preferentially hydrolyzed olive oil, others tributyrin, while most showed no improved selectivity or lost activity. The wild-type was active on both substrates without distinction.
process_signals: Automated screening using liquid-handler robot for higher throughput
Use of saturated inoculum for reproducibility
Qualitative halo analysis: yes/no response
Variants with altered selectivity identified (e.g., B6 prefers olive oil; D4, D6, G9 prefer tributyrin)
Majority of variants showed no improved selectivity or lost activity
Wild-type active on both substrates
methods: Automated whole-cell screening on agar plates with emulsified triglycerides
failures_or_limits: Majority of variants did not show improved selectivity
Some variants lost activity on both substrates
Method is qualitative, not quantitative due to variability in colony size

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: The method offers maximum versatility in generating and screening smart libraries. Golden Gate enables quick library combination and separate treatment of gene parts with different mutagenesis strategies. It allows exploring epistasis and does not require engineering restriction sites. The method was successfully applied to alter Cal-A selectivity as a proof of concept. It can be extended to more than three parts.
process_signals: Method provides versatility in library generation and screening
Golden Gate allows easy combination and separate mutagenesis
Proof of concept: altered selectivity of Cal-A
Potential to extend to more parts
failures_or_limits: Proof of concept only; further optimization may be needed

section_gists.heading: 1. Materials, strains, vectors and culture conditions
section_gists.gist: Standard molecular biology reagents and equipment used. E. coli BL21 (DE3) expression host. Original pelB-Cal-A construct donated. Codon-optimized parts in mother vectors (pM269) and daughter vector (pD441pelB) purchased from DNA2.0. Sequencing performed externally.
process_signals: Use of commercial vectors and codon optimization
pelB leader for periplasmic expression
Initial Tyr93 NDT library generated from donated construct, later regenerated in new vectors
methods: Codon-optimized gene parts in mother/daughter vectors

section_gists.heading: 2. Assembly of plasmids and library generation
section_gists.gist: Restriction digestion and ligation performed routinely. Mutants generated by circular mutagenesis (QuikChange) or error-prone PCR. Assembly designed in silico. Library properties calculated using PEDEL-AA. Ligation mixes transformed into E. coli BL21 (DE3).
process_signals: Use of QuikChange for site-directed mutagenesis
Error-prone PCR for random mutagenesis
In silico design with SnapGene
Library quality assessed by sequencing and PEDEL-AA

section_gists.heading: 3. Screening of Cal-A variants through automation by liquid handler robot
section_gists.gist: Automated screening adapted from manual methods. Tributyrin and olive oil/rhodamine plates prepared. Robot inoculates saturated cultures onto agar plates. Incubation overnight at 30°C. Halos visualized by gel imager. Method is qualitative and robust but not quantitative due to variability in colony size. Negative control with unrelated gene showed no activity.
process_signals: Automation of manual screening method
Use of saturated cultures for reproducibility
Qualitative halo analysis
Negative control confirmed specificity
methods: Automated liquid handling (Beckman Coulter Biomek NX^P)
Agar plate assays with emulsified triglycerides
failures_or_limits: Variability in colony size affects halo appearance

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research aimed to develop a flexible, efficient method for generating smart enzyme libraries by combining Golden Gate assembly with automated screening.
The hypothesis was that independent mutagenesis of gene segments and seamless recombination would allow exploration of synergistic mutations and improve substrate selectivity.
Cal-A was chosen as a model due to its industrial relevance and undefined substrate binding, making it a good test case for the method.
The study likely progressed from method development to proof-of-concept demonstration, with screening revealing variants with altered selectivity, validating the approach.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Were any iterative rounds of mutagenesis and screening performed beyond the initial library?
What were the specific mutations in the improved variants, and were they characterized further?
Did the method encounter any unexpected technical challenges during assembly or screening?
How does the method compare quantitatively to existing library generation methods in terms of efficiency and diversity?
Are there plans to apply the method to other enzymes or to further optimize Cal-A selectivity?

events.summary: Existing smart library methods suffer from high cost, experimental demand, time consumption, and lack of flexibility in recombining mutations.
Cal-A gene divided into three parts for independent mutagenesis: part 1 (N-terminal, residues 11-210), part 2 (tunnel region, 211-350), part 3 (C-terminal, 351-end).
Targeted NDT saturation mutagenesis chosen for Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431; random mutagenesis (error-prone PCR) for tunnel region (part 2).
Golden Gate assembly with BsaI and SapI designed for seamless recombination of parts into expression vector, allowing various library combinations.
Ten libraries generated with various combinations of mutated and native parts, yielding at least 10^3 transformants each.
Library quality assessed by sequencing pooled clones, confirming expected diversity.
Use of circularized daughter plasmid for library Tyr93-Phe431 yielded no transformants; resolved by using commercial pre-cut plasmid.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers library design and assembly, including problem statement, hypothesis, design choices, and initial validation/failure.

events.summary: Quality of NDT libraries assessed by sequencing pooled clones, confirming expected codon degeneracy.
Quality of randomly mutated library assessed by sequencing 20 clones, revealing mutational load and distribution.
Screening of Cal-A libraries against tributyrin (C4) and olive oil/rhodamine (C18) to evaluate selectivity.
Screening revealed variants with altered selectivity; wild-type active on both substrates without distinction.
Majority of variants showed no improved selectivity or lost activity on both substrates.
Mutation at Tyr93 retained activity in 89% of variants with preference for long-chain; Tyr183 crucial for activity with only 10% active and preference for short-chain.
Results demonstrate suitability of Golden Gate strategy for rapid generation of recombined functional diversity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of library quality, screening design, experimental results, and validation of the method.

events.summary: Screening assay uses tributyrin plates for short-chain activity and olive oil/rhodamine plates for long-chain activity.
Robot script designed to inoculate agar plates with 8 μL of saturated culture from 96-well plates.
Halo analysis is qualitative, giving a yes/no response for activity; method is robust but not quantitative due to variability in colony size.
Negative control using E. coli expressing an unrelated gene showed no activity, confirming specificity.
Simplified one-pot assembly strategy had reduced success rate for mutant parts, so standard method was used.
Colony PCR and sequencing confirmed 100% correct ligation products for recombined libraries.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk details the automated screening method, its qualitative nature, and technical aspects of library assembly including a failure of the simplified one-pot method and validation of assembly success.
This chunk contains only reference citations and no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: These methods typically suffer from one or more disadvantage including high cost, being experimentally demanding, requiring time consuming steps or, importantly, not providing flexibility in recombining mutations.
The three ‘parts’ of Cal-A were synthesized in a codon-optimized form
We chose NDT degeneracy at these three positions
the entire tunnel region (residues 211-350) was targeted for random mutagenesis
The junctions between parts were designed with a unique BsaI restriction site while two unique SapI restriction sites were designed to ligate the assembled gene into the daughter vector
We thus rapidly obtained a variety of complex, mutated libraries, ready to bring forward to screening (Table 1).
The quality of each of the degenerate NDT libraries, whether resulting from mutation of one part or assembly of more than one mutated part, was routinely assessed by sequencing pooled clones.
In one instance (construction of library Tyr93-Phe431), use of the circularized daughter plasmid afforded no transformants. This issue was resolved by using the commercial, pre-cut daughter plasmid
codon degeneracy can be clearly visible in the DNA sequencing electropherogram (Fig 3), confirming the expected distribution of nucleotides instead of the original codon.
The quality of the randomly mutated library was assessed by sequencing 20 clones (Table 2).

The 20 clones carried a total mutational load of 34, with approximately 25% of the clones being wild-type and the majority of clones carrying two mutations (average = 1.65), for a maximum of 4 mutations per clone.
The resulting Cal-A libraries expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) were screened to evaluate selectivity of hydrolase activity toward long versus short-chain triglycerides.
variant B6 hydrolyzes olive oil preferentially over tributyrin, while variants D4, D6 and G9 show the inverse selectivity.
the wild-type was active with tributyrin and olive oil without distinction.

The majority of the variants do not show any improved selectivity compared to the wild-type Cal-A (e.g. A2, A3, the majority of row B, etc, relative to wild-type A11, starred) or they lose the ability to hydrolyze both of the substrates (A1, A4, A5, and so on).
When Tyr93 was mutated, 89% of the clones retained activity and discriminated preferentially towards long-chain fatty acids.
In contrast, Tyr183 was crucial for activity: when mutated, only 10% of the variants retained activity, with a preference for the hydrolysis of short-chain fatty acids.
These results demonstrate the suitability of the Golden Gate strategy to achieve rapid generation of readily recombined functional diversity.
For the olive/oil rhodamine plates, the tributyrin was replaced with 2.5 g olive oil, and rhodamine was added at a concentration of 0.001% w/v.
A script was designed to pick up 20 μL of culture, column by column, from the inoculum plates and spot 8 μL of inoculum by lightly touching the agar surface.
Halo analysis is qualitative, giving a yes/no response as to activity of a variant toward a specific substrate.
The method is qualitatively robust, in that an active variant consistently shows a halo and a negative variant consistently does not

the current method does not lend itself to quantitative activity measurements because of factors including insufficient precision in the number of cells inoculated which affects colony size and thus appearance of the halo
As a negative control, a culture of E. coli BL21 (DE3) harboring an inducible, unrelated gene (cytochrome P450 BM3) was tested under the same conditions.

Upon screening more than 15 randomly-selected clones for each recombined library, we observed 100% correct ligation products by colony PCR.

By sequencing approximately 50 clones from each library, we identified 11 of the 12 codons expected for the Tyr93 libraries, 10 of 12 for the Tyr183 libraries and 9 of 12 for the Phe431 libraries.

While previously reported in the context of shuffling native genes,[19] we apply the strategy to further increase sequence diversity, by combining synthetically mutated gene segments into complex, smart libraries[17, 19, 21-24].

PART 1 (N-terminal region 11–210, in green) is comprised of the $\alpha/\beta$ fold and includes Tyr93 and Tyr183.

Based on further structural analysis (PDB identification code: 2VEO)[26] and proximity to the catalytic triad (within 5 Å from the hydroxyl oxygen of Ser183), residues Tyr93, Tyr183 and Phe431 were selected as potential hot-spots for mutagenesis.

Cal-A is an unusual lipase: it is stable at high temperatures ( $>90^{\circ}C$ ) and at acidic pH, accepts sterically hindered and tertiary alcohols as substrates, shows a preference for $S_{N}2$ hydrolysis of triglycerides, offers selectivity towards trans-fatty acids and can accept amino acids and amino esters as substrates $[25–27]$ .
On the deck of the robot, four 96-well plates containing 1 mL aliquots of the library variants pre-grown to saturation (LB, overnight, shaking, 37°C) served as inoculum for the agar plates.
The screening of the variants against tributyrin and olive oil was based on previously reported techniques[31, 32].
Library properties calculated at the PEDEL-AA server page[34] assumed a Poisson distribution[35].

DNA sequencing was performed by the Genomic Platform of IRIC (Institute for Research in Immunology and Cancerology), Université de Montréal, except for sequencing of the Tyr183 and Phe431 libraries that was performed by the Centre d’Innovation Génome Québec at McGill University (QC, Canada).
Phe431 tolerated mutation without little effect, as activity was maintained in 90% of the variants and no increased discrimination was found.

Lipases have been screened in the past using robust in-plate assays involving either tributyrin or olive oil/rhodamine emulsions in agar: plates were inoculated with lipase variants, and hydrolytic activity could be detected upon triglyceride hydrolysis either with the formation of a halo of clearance (in the case of tributyrin) or a fluorescent rhodamine halo upon fatty acid release due to a change in pH[31, 33].
Transformation of the mutant libraries yielded at least $10^{3}$ transformants for each library: between 80 and 100% of the colonies contained the desired constructs.

We chose NDT degeneracy at these three positions (where N = A,C,G,T, and D = A, G or T; covering 12 codons, 12 amino acids) as it provides good chemical diversity while keeping library size modest[29].

When ready to be recombined, for example upon library generation, the parts were reassembled into the complete gene in an Electra daughter vector (pD441pelB, DNA2.
To increase the likelihood of modulating Cal-A substrate specificity, we sought potential synergistic effects that required combining libraries of the above mutations[1, 30].

Mutation of the bulky Tyr93, located below the catalytic triad, could modulate substrate access; removal of the bulky Tyr183 appears to open a putative tunnel (confirmed by molecular dynamics simulations; results not shown).

The long chain of a C18 fatty acid substrate has been hypothesized to bind in a tunnel where PEG crystallized, and targeted NDT mutagenesis of that region had previously shown some effect on cis-trans substrate selectivity[25].
Citation: Quaglia D, Ebert MCCJC, Mugford PF, Pelletier JN (2017) Enzyme engineering: A synthetic biology approach for more effective library generation and automated high-throughput screening.

DNA sequencing of a number of those clones served to assess the library quality (refer to text in main paper and Table 2).
This construct was used to produce the initial Tyr93 NDT library.

As a result, this method provides a means to create highly mutated portions of genes that can be inserted either into the wild-type background (see Library 10) or recombined together (Libraries 8, 9) such that epistasis among residues within specified regions can readily be explored.
In the representative example of screened plates presented in Fig 4, variant B6 hydrolyzes olive oil preferentially over tributyrin, while variants D4, D6 and G9 show the inverse selectivity.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Develop a versatile method for generating and screening smart enzyme libraries to engineer Candida antarctica lipase A (Cal-A) for altered substrate selectivity between short-chain and long-chain fatty acid triglycerides.

canonical_events.summary: Existing smart library methods suffer from high cost, experimental demand, time consumption, and lack of flexibility in recombining mutations.
Cal-A gene divided into three parts for independent mutagenesis: part 1 (N-terminal, residues 11-210), part 2 (tunnel region, 211-350), part 3 (C-terminal, 351-end).
Targeted NDT saturation mutagenesis chosen for Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431; random mutagenesis (error-prone PCR) for tunnel region (part 2).
Golden Gate assembly with BsaI and SapI designed for seamless recombination of parts into expression vector, allowing various library combinations.
Ten libraries generated with various combinations of mutated and native parts, yielding at least 10^3 transformants each.
Use of circularized daughter plasmid for library Tyr93-Phe431 yielded no transformants; resolved by using commercial pre-cut plasmid.
Library quality assessed by sequencing pooled clones, confirming expected diversity for NDT libraries and mutational load/distribution for random library.
Screening of Cal-A libraries against tributyrin (C4) and olive oil/rhodamine (C18) to evaluate selectivity using automated whole-cell assay.
Screening revealed variants with altered selectivity; wild-type active on both substrates without distinction. Majority of variants showed no improved selectivity or lost activity.

Mutation at Tyr93 retained activity in 89% of variants with preference for long-chain; Tyr183 crucial for activity with only 10% active and preference for short-chain; Phe431 tolerated mutation without effect on selectivity.
Simplified one-pot assembly strategy had reduced success rate for mutant parts, so standard method was used.
Colony PCR and sequencing confirmed 100% correct ligation products for recombined libraries.
Halo analysis is qualitative, giving a yes/no response for activity; method is robust but not quantitative due to variability in colony size.
Results demonstrate suitability of Golden Gate strategy for rapid generation of recombined functional diversity, successfully applied to alter Cal-A selectivity as proof of concept.

edges.reason: Problem of inflexibility in recombining mutations motivates dividing gene into parts for independent mutagenesis.
Gene division allows targeted and random mutagenesis strategies on specific parts.
Mutagenesis strategies require assembly method to recombine parts; Golden Gate designed for seamless recombination.
Golden Gate assembly enables generation of ten library combinations.
During library generation, a failure occurred with circularized plasmid for one library.
After library generation, quality was assessed by sequencing.
Screening results are analyzed to identify variants with altered selectivity.
Detailed analysis of mutation effects on activity and selectivity.
During screening method development, one-pot assembly failure was encountered.
Standard method validated by colony PCR and sequencing.
Altered selectivity variants validate the Golden Gate strategy for functional diversity.

must_cover_points: Identification of variants with altered selectivity
